settings.title: Settings
settings.resetConfirm: Are you sure? All progress will be deleted!
settings.aboutText: Mental Math is an educational game for kids. It teaches mental calculation through 12 phases, from addition to multiplication near 10.

achievement.streakMsg: You answered {n} questions correctly in a row!

1.title: Friends of Number 10
1.subtitle: Learn which numbers make 10
1.idea: The number 10 is the first big 'nice number'. If you know which combinations make 10, adding, subtracting, multiplying and dividing become much easier.
1.mantra: Numbers have friends. When two friends come together, we get the nice number 10.
1.parentTip: Use Lego bricks, candies, pebbles or fingers. If the child is guessing, go back to physical objects.

2.title: Single-Digit Addition
2.subtitle: Add without counting from 1
2.idea: Keep the bigger number in your head and add the smaller one. You don't need to count from the beginning every time.
2.mantra: Keep the bigger number, add the smaller one.
2.parentTip: Ask: 'What's easier: counting everything from the start or keeping the bigger number in your head?'

3.title: Addition Beyond 10
3.subtitle: Make 10, then continue
3.idea: When a number is close to 10, help it become 10. Then the calculation is easier.
3.parentTip: Important question: 'How did you know that?' The child should be able to say: 'I made 10.'

4.title: Basic Subtraction
4.subtitle: Take away a part, see what's left
4.idea: Subtraction means: I have something, I take away a part, I see how much is left.
4.mantra: Take away a part, see what's left.
4.parentTip: Put blocks on the table, remove some, ask how many are left. Connect to addition: if 6 + 4 = 10, then 10 - 6 = 4.

5.title: Subtraction as Distance
5.subtitle: Jump to 10, then to the goal
5.idea: Sometimes we don't need to move anything. We just look at how far one number is from another.
5.mantra: Jump to 10, then to the goal.
5.parentTip: For 13 - 8: 'How far is it from 8 to 13?' 8 to 10 is 2, 10 to 13 is 3, total 5.

6.title: Two-Digit Addition
6.subtitle: Tens first, then ones
6.idea: A two-digit number has a big part (tens) and a small part (ones). First combine the big parts, then the small ones.
6.parentTip: For 42 + 35: first 40 + 30 = 70, then 2 + 5 = 7, total 77. It's more natural for the brain to first know roughly where the answer is.

7.title: Addition by Rounding
7.subtitle: Give a little to one number to make it nice
7.idea: Turn one number into a nice number (20, 30, 40, 50), and adjust the other. It's not cheating, it's smart calculating!
7.mantra: Give a little to one number to make it nice.
7.parentTip: For 29 + 16: 29 needs 1 to reach 30. Take 1 from 16, leaving 15. 30 + 15 = 45. This teaches the child to see numbers as flexible.

8.title: Multiplication as Groups
8.subtitle: Equal groups
8.idea: Multiplication is not a table to memorize. Multiplication means: multiple equal groups.
8.parentTip: Use plates and cookies. 'If we have 3 plates and 4 cookies on each plate, how many cookies do we have?'

9.title: Double, Half, ×5 and ×10
9.subtitle: Smart multiplication
9.idea: Some tables don't need to be memorized. They can be understood through doubling, halving and tens.
9.mantra: ×2 is double. ×4 is double-double. ×5 is half of ×10. ×10 is easy.
9.parentTip: For 7 × 5: first 7 × 10 = 70, then half of 70 is 35. The child sees that multiplication isn't magic.

10.title: Sharing Things
10.subtitle: Share fairly
10.idea: Division means: we have something and we share it fairly. Everyone gets the same amount.
10.parentTip: Put 12 blocks and 3 plates. The child places blocks one by one until all plates have the same amount.

11.title: Division as Inverse Multiplication
11.subtitle: If I know multiplication, I know division
11.idea: Division and multiplication are connected. One number family gives 4 calculations.
11.mantra: If I know multiplication, I know division.
11.parentTip: Number family: 3, 4, 12. From them: 3×4=12, 4×3=12, 12÷3=4, 12÷4=3. This develops deep understanding.

12.title: Speed Math Tricks
12.subtitle: Multiplication near 10
12.idea: When multiplying numbers close to 10, we ask: 'How far from 10?' Subtract crosswise, multiply the small numbers.
12.mantra: How far from 10? Subtract crosswise. Multiply the small numbers.
12.parentTip: DON'T teach this too early! The child must first understand 'how far from 10'. For 8×9: 8 needs 2, 9 needs 1. 8-1=7, 2×1=2. Answer: 72.
